author: Joe Kirchner
tone: Formal, analytical, cautionary, respectful but firm
executive_summary: Joe Kirchner opposes the SEC's proposed rescission of Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) and Rule 610(e) (prohibition on locking and crossing quotations) under Regulation NMS. His central argument is that the rapid proliferation of AI and machine-learning systems in trading makes these bright-line protections more necessary, not less. He contends that best-execution obligations are too subjective and opaque to substitute for Rule 611's objective, machine-verifiable benchmark in an AI-driven market environment. He raises concerns about retail investor harm, degraded price discovery, liquidity migration to dark venues, and unpredictable emergent behavior from AI systems operating in an unguarded market structure. He urges the Commission to withdraw the proposed amendments and conduct robust AI-specific empirical analysis before pursuing any reforms.
market_structure_topics: Prohibition on locking and crossing quotations (Rule 610(e)) rescission
AI and machine-learning in trading and order routing
Best execution as a regulatory standard
Emergent behavior and feedback loops in AI-driven markets

positions.order_competition_rule: Strongly supports retaining Rule 611; argues it provides an objective, machine-verifiable benchmark that is especially critical in AI-driven markets where subjective standards are harder to monitor and enforce
positions.best_execution: Skeptical that best execution obligations can substitute for Rule 611; characterizes best execution as a subjective, principles-based standard that is inadequate for overseeing opaque, adaptive AI algorithms operating at microsecond speed
positions.transparency: Implicitly supports transparency; argues that removing Rule 611 shifts oversight to opaque, adaptive algorithms that regulators, brokers, and investors cannot readily reconstruct or audit, widening the accountability gap

economic_arguments: The Commission has not demonstrated that compliance-cost savings from rescission would offset the risks created by removing these protections
Empirical analysis derived from a pre-AI market structure cannot be assumed to hold in markets now shaped by machine intelligence
The proposal lacks robust analysis specifically addressing AI-driven trading dynamics and their interaction with the rescinded rules
Wholesale rescission is disproportionate; narrower, targeted reforms should be considered instead
liquidity_arguments: Rule 611 rewards participants who post the best displayed prices by ensuring those quotes are honored, incentivizing public quote display
Removing Rule 611 could accelerate migration of liquidity into opaque venues as AI quoting strategies deprioritize public display
Degraded public quote display would harm price discovery for all market participants
Permitting locked and crossed markets under Rule 610(e) rescission could produce emergent, hard-to-predict AI behavior and feedback loops that the Commission has not analyzed
competition_arguments: AI-driven trading sharpens the asymmetry between sophisticated firms and ordinary investors
Firms deploying advanced models to predict order flow and optimize execution stand to benefit most from removal of a baseline price guarantee
Retail and less-sophisticated investors cannot monitor execution quality across dozens of venues in real time or audit routing algorithms, making them disproportionately vulnerable
Rule 611 is a safeguard that does not depend on an investor's technological resources to be effective, leveling the playing field
retail_investor_arguments: Retail investors lack the ability to monitor execution quality across multiple venues in real time
Retail investors cannot audit the algorithms routing their orders
A bright-line protection like Rule 611 is precisely the kind of safeguard that protects investors regardless of their technological sophistication or resources
Removing Rule 611 shifts the burden of protection onto investors least equipped to bear it
recommended_actions: Withdraw the proposed amendments to rescind Rule 611 and Rule 610(e)
Conduct robust empirical analysis specifically addressing AI-driven trading dynamics before pursuing any reforms
Study how autonomous and increasingly adaptive trading systems would behave once these guardrails are removed
Consider narrower, targeted reforms rather than wholesale rescission of Regulation NMS protections
key_quotes: The rapid integration of artificial intelligence into trading and order routing makes these protections more necessary, not less.

Best execution is a subjective, principles-based standard, and in an environment where routing, pricing, and liquidity decisions are increasingly made by AI and machine-learning systems operating at microsecond speed, subjective standards are far harder to monitor and enforce.
The Order Protection Rule offers an objective, machine-verifiable benchmark — a displayed price that either was or was not honored.
Removing it would shift oversight from a clear rule to the inspection of opaque, adaptive algorithms whose decision logic regulators, brokers, and investors cannot readily reconstruct or audit.
A bright-line protection like Rule 611 is precisely the kind of safeguard that does not depend on an investor's technological resources to be effective.
Removing that assurance could accelerate the migration of liquidity into opaque venues and incentivize models to deprioritize public quote display, degrading price discovery for everyone.
AI systems optimizing within an environment of locked and crossed quotations may produce emergent, hard-to-predict behavior and feedback loops that the Commission has not analyzed.
Empirical analysis from a pre-AI market structure cannot be assumed to hold in markets now shaped by machine intelligence.
Before dismantling protections that have underpinned U.S. equity markets for nearly two decades, the Commission should conduct robust analysis specifically addressing AI-driven trading dynamics.
